data.title: Dip-Buyers Ride Longest Negative Signal Since 2022 To Next Tactical Bottom
data.description:
Introduction

Death, taxes, and retail traders buying the dip.

Those used to be the only certainties in life. Throughout 2025, even when institutional investors were feeling sheepish, day traders could be relied upon to step in and backstop a sell-off.

Dip-buying basically became their identity. A year-end review from JPMorgan found that - in their most active year to date - retail investors amassed 75% of their positions during buy-the-dip episodes. Not this time. In fact, JPMorgan found that retail traders were net sellers of stocks for the first time in nine months. What a heel turn! They didn't buy the dip … they sold the rip." ~ Joe Ciolli, Business Insider, March 2026

Timing matters, and it matters greatly. I have spent the last 35 years trading, researching, and constructing algorithms to identify and leverage the value across fundamental, technical, and behavioral finance models. Of the ten portfolio models designed for optimal portfolio mixes for members to beat the market at Value & Momentum Breakouts, eight come from enhancing well-tested anomaly research in published financial journals. All of the models continue to outperform the S&P 500 in live forward testing here on Seeking Alpha, and again this year.

Dip-Buyers Ride Longest Negative Signal Since 2022 To Next Tactical Bottom

As members of my investing community know well, there has been a rotation underway since last October. While the headlines now at the end of March blare sirens of the worst weekly losing streak since 2022, we have been writing and posting to signal readers for months. Next, I'm going to talk about "tactical bottoms" and I don't mean military pants in the online stores.

Let me boil it down to the two most important Momentum Gauge signals from my doctoral finance research. If you are learning about this for the first time, I will keep it at the high level and include several recent articles for you to consider here from the past few months:

  • Echoes Of 2022: Bear Bounces As ETF Signal Portfolio Leads S&P 500 By Over 23%
  • If This Is A 2022 Market Repeat, Here Is What Likely Happens Next
  • First Negative S&P 500 Signals As Mega Tech Breaks Down From October Highs

As the State Street SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY) and MicroSectors FANG+ 3X Leveraged ETN (FNGU) fund charts illustrate below, we have been in a significant downturn and rotation for the past 6 months with strong similarities to 2022 from my prior articles and posts.

These key funds translate to the two most important momentum gauges, in my opinion, for forecasting the Major Indices that are most heavily weighted on technology and semiconductors.

The S&P 500

First, the S&P 500 represents the 500 largest stocks in the US market and is an excellent way to assess overall equity money flows. However, it is actually a terrible way to determine how well 11 different sectors are performing in the market of several thousand stocks not on any major index.

As you can see on the weekly gauges, we have been in a negative signal for the past 9 weeks, starting the week of January 30th. I have shared dozens of posts, alerts, articles, and updates since then to help readers mitigate the weakness. At the end of the article, I will show how we have traded these signals for strong Q1 gains.

Technology Sector Gauge

The technology sector is the largest and most heavily weighted sector on the major indices and deserves the most attention for the direction of the "Market" for those trading the popular indices like Nasdaq (QQQ) and S&P 500 (SPY).

The daily Technology gauges have been negative since January 14th, the weekly Technology gauges have been negative since January 16th, and the monthly gauges shown below have been negative since October 2025.

The technology gauges correspond closely with the MicroSectors FANG+ 3x Leveraged ETNs shown below of 10 of the largest stocks in the market heavily weighted on technology. The bearish stair steps of this chart have been shared dozens of times with readers since last year, highlighting the strong similarities to 2022 with many bear bounces. Bear bounces are essentially the strong conviction of dip-buyers that a bottom has arrived, while institutional money managers are still reducing their exposure to the markets in a disciplined channel of selling points. This process has been unfolding since October in the largest stocks in the market and may be nearing a "tactical bottom" for a real dip-buying opportunity soon.

Now with MicroSectors FANG+ 3x Leveraged ETNs (FNGU) down over -57% from its highs, we are actually seeing some capitulation spreading over to NVIDIA Corp. (NVDA) in the most recent MDA [multiple-discriminant analysis] stock chart. NVIDIA is highly significant for any market recovery and, in a recent estimate represent 30% more market cap than the entire two thousand stocks in the Russell 2000 combined. NVIDIA is basically an index of its own.

I share this chart frequently, as well as the semiconductor bull fund chart Direxion Daily Semiconductor Bull 3X ETF (SOXL) to help people know when a real bottom for the Major Indices has arrived. You can see this more detailed analysis in my prior article. It is very difficult for the Market Indices to find a bottom without the participation of the semiconductors and NVIDIA in particular. So that will be one of the key indicators of when it will be safer to load up in the general market indices.

The Next Tactical Bottom

So if the dip-buyers have begun to capitulate, it is also likely that the institutional sellers are nearing the end of their market exposure reductions. The main consideration about market bottoms is that they rarely occur equally across all market sectors. I use the phrase tactical bottom as it presents a way to start identifying positive opportunities even when bottoms are not precisely neat and well defined.

From the top of my automated dashboard we can get a summary of all the momentum gauges across 11 sectors, the entire market, and the S&P 500. This summary lets us observe easily where the tactical bottom is starting to improve first.

If I had to summarize it, and I often do, I would look for 3 things to call a market bottom. Most people define the market as the "Market Indices" that exclude many thousands of stocks. So this is what I would look for:1. The S&P 500 and Technology gauges turning positive again.

2. A majority of sector gauges turning positive, including a majority of the 3 mega cap sectors, Communications, Technology, and Consumer Cyclicals.

3. At least 2 consecutive days of market gains and three consecutive days of rising positive gauge values.

Or you could watch how these different gauges come off near bottoms for a rebound into Q2:

How do we trade the signals?

Since joining Seeking Alpha over 9 years ago to live-test various financial portfolio models I have applied both the buy/hold approach and the MG timing approach. My long term value, forensic, and dividend portfolios are shown in the most basic buy/hold returns not adjusted for dividends. My active portfolios have been adjusting each year to different daily, weekly, monthly indicators to show enhanced returns to members.

While the major indices are sharply lower and have a bit further to fall before a positive signal returns, many of our trading portfolios remain positive even without following the gauges in a riskier buy/hold approach.

This year I added a CFO insider trading portfolio and a new Monthly ETF signal portfolio. While these returns only reflect the January portfolio returns, I also release mid-year portfolios that members can use to refresh their annual portfolios every July if they choose.

So if we look at the daily S&P 500 gauge chart back to January 2025, we can see that the positive gauge values in green are hitting max low values near 0 that we have not seen since the March bottom last year.

This bottoming signal tells us that capitulation has arrived and it generally does not last for more than a few weeks. We should expect some big bear bounces to test support near these levels, but do not expect a market recovery without Technology, semiconductors or NVIDIA also turning positive.

  • Echoes Of 2022: Bear Bounces As ETF Signal Portfolio Leads S&P 500 By Over 23%

So following the signals this year, we have been in cash from February locking in gains to start the year. Nearly all the portfolios are enhanced by following the signals that have avoided the downturns this year.

Among these long-term portfolios, the new Active Monthly ETF that trades in both bull/bear funds across 11 sectors and the market and S&P 500 gauges is up +21.1% YTD leading the S&P 500 by over +27% in Q1.

Two funds for each different sector, as well as the S&P 500 and Market gauge are represented in the monthly portfolio, with the next update for April out next week. The leading funds in the portfolio are highlighted below as the first quarter of the year comes to a close.

Following the signals, for example, the energy sector gauge signal has kept us in energy bull funds like Direxion Daily Energy Bull 2X ETF (ERX), State Street Energy Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLE) from the start of the year and S&P 500 bear funds like ProShares UltraPro Short S&P 500 (SPXU) from February.

I submit that following signals will enhance your returns. You can use any portfolio that you prefer from any source you trust. At a minimum, you can protect your downside while compensated financial institutions keep insisting that you should stay in the market and be more patient while your portfolios sell off. One member observed that going with aggressive portfolios in the positive signals delivered large returns and they could switch to bear funds in negative signals following either the weekly or monthly ETF portfolios for additional gains. That approach could deliver even larger gains than I have posted in any single portfolio this year.

FINRA cautions investors that:

Exchange-traded funds (ETFs) that offer leverage or that are designed to perform inversely to the index or benchmark they track, or both, are growing in number and popularity. While such products may be useful in some sophisticated trading strategies, they are highly complex financial instruments that are typically designed to achieve their stated objectives on a daily basis. Due to the effects of compounding, their performance over longer periods of time can differ significantly from their stated daily objective. Therefore, inverse and leveraged ETFs that are reset daily typically are unsuitable for retail investors who plan to hold them for longer than one trading session, particularly in volatile markets.

Conclusion

Whatever you choose, there are many strong portfolios and opportunities for trading in this volatility. Over the years, I have found that timing matters greatly.

Scholars have documented for years that "harvesting different risk baskets, each with a premium that has low-to-negative correlations to the others," is an excellent way to produce strong risk-adjusted returns. These ongoing sector rotations and signal changes continue to give us many new opportunities for profit in the years ahead as we build our optimal portfolio mixes each year.

I always encourage considering three steps in stock selection, whether for long-term value or short-term gains:

  1. Check the direction of the Market Gauges for the largest positive flows.
  2. Identify the Sector Gauges that are most positive and least extended to further enhance the probabilities of gains.
  3. Lastly, confirm the positive MDA breakout signals on individual stocks in the best sector and best market conditions to maximize returns.

I wish you well in all your trading decisions,

JD Henning, PhD.

data.title: Enterprise Products Partners: The Window Of Opportunity Is Closing Fast (Downgrade)
data.description:
I last wrote about Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) at the end of last October, rating it a buy and highlighting that it is coming up to a cash flow inflection point and that the recent headwinds, which had weighed on its stock price, were temporary. Thus, I felt that its 7.2% next twelve-month yield, its EV/EBITDA ratio below ten times, and its upcoming cash flow catalyst made it an attractive buy at the moment, with its richest chapter potentially ahead of it.

That call turned out to be prescient, as since that article was published, EPD has delivered a total return of 29%, while the S&P 500 (SPY) has declined by about 7.5%. However, given that strong outperformance, its next 12-month yield has declined to about 5.5%, and its enterprise value to EBITDA multiple has increased substantially to about 11.5 times on a next 12-month basis. As a result, the valuation is not as compelling as it once was. In this article, I am going to reassess the investment thesis in the wake of its Q4 results and its increased valuation to determine if it is still a buy or if a downgrade is in order.

Record 2025 Results and Q4 Bounce Back

The company's Q4 2025 results indicated a strong bounce back from its Q3 weakness, as its gross operating margin increased by 4.1% year over year and was up 14.8% sequentially from a weak Q3 2025. Contributing to this were:

  • The Frac14 coming online, which had been delayed for three months

  • The Neches River Terminal Phase One coming online and contributing a full amount during the quarter

  • Strong performance from the natural gas segment and Permian Basin gathering growth

All told, EPD generated a record $10 billion in adjusted full-year 2025 EBITDA, along with adjusted cash flow from operations of $8.7 billion.

A Fortress Balance Sheet in Midstream

Looking ahead, EPD remains in a very strong fundamental position, with one of the most integrated midstream systems in North America across NGL pipelines, fractionation, LPG and ethane export terminals, natural gas gathering, processing, and pipeline infrastructure, crude oil pipelines, and marine terminals, with a full-value chain that EPD controls from the wellhead to the water.

The company also generates pretty stable fee-based cash flows, with 82% of its cash flows being fee-based, 13% differential-based, and 5% commodity price-based. Additionally, about 90% of its long-term contracts include inflation escalation provisions. Perhaps most noteworthy is the fact that, with an A- credit rating from S&P, EPD stands out among all of its midstream peers, including in the C corporation sector, as having the highest credit rating.

This credit rating is justified by its 3.3 times leverage ratio, which is expected to decline to 3.0 times or even lower as project EBITDA comes online in the coming quarters and excess cash flow is used to pay down debt. Additionally, with $5.2 billion of liquidity and a 17-year weighted average time to maturity on its debt, with 98% of its debt at fixed interest rates, EPD has very little risk of going into financial distress.

2027 Shaping Up as a Breakout Growth Year

EPD's growth outlook is as promising as ever, with the company expecting its growth capex to peak at $4.4 billion in 2025, declining to between $2.5 billion and $2.9 billion this year and to $2 billion to $2.5 billion in 2027. Additionally, with $4.8 billion worth of major projects currently under construction, with most of them expected to enter service this year, 2027 is set up to be a major growth year, with the company forecasting about 10% year-over-year EBITDA growth in 2027 after growing EBITDA at a low to mid-single-digit pace this year.

It's also worth knowing that this growth guidance was issued before the war in Iran, with energy prices soaring, which will likely boost EPD's EBITDA further this year and is also setting up potential growth tailwinds in the coming years. In fact, analysts forecast a 6.2% distributable cash flow per unit CAGR through 2030 and a 4.1% distribution per unit CAGR over that same period, which will likely see further upside due to the ramifications of the war in Iran.

Why the Iran War Could Be EPD's Biggest Unexpected Catalyst

Wells Fargo already upgraded EPD due to expected strong LPG export demand, as global buyers are likely going to turn to the US increasingly to diversify away from riskier Middle Eastern energy supplies that must transit the Strait of Hormuz. In particular, Asian allies like Japan, South Korea, and Taiwan will likely turn to Alaska for a more stable energy supply, while Europe may turn to the US increasingly as well. EPD is positioned to be a big winner from this, given its LPG export strength.

Why a Compelling Buy Has Become a Hold

While the yield is still attractive at around 5.5% on a next 12-month basis and the coverage ratio remains quite sound, with strong growth expected for both distributable cash flow per unit and the distribution itself, as well as potential unit repurchases as free cash flow accelerates, I'm less bullish on EPD than I used to be. There are several reasons for this:

  • Its EV/EBITDA has increased significantly to where it now stands at 11.47 times, which is well above its five-year average of 9.56 times and even above its 10-year average of 10.85 times.

  • Even a more bullish analyst at Wells Fargo has EPD with a price target of $42, which implies fairly pedestrian single-digit percentage upside from here.

  • EPD plans to use about 50% of its excess free cash flow towards buybacks, which are now much less accretive than they would have been previously, making this a less attractive use of capital. Instead, I would favor an approach of paying out supplemental distributions or even growing the distribution more rapidly than they would otherwise. Perhaps EPD will shift their strategy if their unit price remains elevated, but thus far they have given no signal of doing that, which means it should not be expected unless they say so.

Valuation Gap With MPLX Has Narrowed, and So Has the Risk-Reward

Overall, I see EPD as having now closed the valuation gap to some degree with peers like MPLX LP (MPLX), and therefore it is no longer the compelling buy that it was last fall. Additionally, EPD faces some risks, including:

  • Continued project execution risk, which could rear its ugly head again through project delays

  • If energy prices plummet in response to the war in Iran being resolved, that could also be a headwind, especially to the unit price, due to broader industry sentiment shifts

  • Capital allocation risk, which has become even greater in the wake of the unit price running up, giving management fewer options for how to allocate capital responsibly outside of returning it to unit holders via distributions

Why I'm Still Glad I Held, But Now Downgrading to Hold

While EPD is not as cheap as it once was, and I am now downgrading it to a hold, EPD has provided valuable diversification for my portfolio during the recent market turmoil in the wake of the Iran war. Thus, it has been a great investment for me, as it has been in my very largest positions in my 7-8% yielding, total return outperforming, and below-market beta portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: POET Technologies: A Scalable Alternative To Nvidia's Laser Supply Lock-Up
data.description:
POET Technologies Inc. (POET) is a name that I have been extremely bullish on, rating it as a strong buy in multiple instances over the past year. Recently, POET’s stock has been under pressure as it is down more than 13% over the past month, despite a successful participation at the OFC 2026 conference. During the conference, POET announced a significant partnership with optoelectronics giant LITEON and an expanded partnership with Lessengers; both are for the co-development of 1.6T transceivers.

With POET expanding its partnerships with Tier 2 ODMs, the company’s moves over the past years appear to have resulted in a CPO ecosystem parallel to Nvidia’s (NVDA). In a world where hyperscalers are utilizing their own ASICs and custom AI infrastructures, these partnerships could allow POET to penetrate hyperscalers’ data centers as they continue to shift away from Nvidia’s complete, closed-loop solutions.

The significance of this ecosystem is already starting to show, in my opinion, after Nvidia’s latest investments in Coherent (COHR) and Lumentum (LITE) amidst the ongoing Indium Phosphide (InP) shortage. As these investments could further tighten laser supply, POET could have a significant moat since its 1.6T optical engines utilize 4 laser chips, potentially allowing its customers to produce more modules at the same global InP supply.

In my opinion, this moat could see POET receiving meaningful orders from its partners, LITEON and Luxshare, setting it up for a meaningful revenue ramp in late 2027. With a strong cash position to expand capacity ahead of this potential demand, I’m reiterating my strong buy rating for POET.

OFC 2026 Highlights

POET is coming off the OFC 2026 conference, where it showcased its external light source (ELS) products, Starlight and Blazar. The company also received the Lightwave Innovation Reviews award for its Teralight optical engine during the conference, which is set to be at the core of its latest partnerships.

POET’s participation in OFC can be described as successful after it secured 2 partnerships during the conference. The first partnership is with LITEON for the co-development of optical modules built on POET’s proprietary optical interposer platform for next-gen co-packaged optics (CPO), AI systems, and high-bandwidth data center applications. Prototypes from this collaboration are expected to be ready by late 2026, with high-volume manufacturing expected in 2027.

Given that this collaboration represents LITEON’s entry into optical transceivers, it is likely that POET will supply optical engines for these transceivers while LITEON will handle the overall design and high-volume manufacturing of these transceivers. Additionally, the fact that this venture represents LITEON’s first time building optical transceivers suggests that this partnership could be stickier than POET’s other partnerships, as it signals that LITEON’s hopes in the transceiver market are highly dependent on POET’s interposer platform and IP.

By bringing its technology and LITEON’s high-volume manufacturing expertise and customer relationships together, this partnership could be a major tailwind for the adoption of POET’s products. As is, LITEON is already a supplier of power management systems and rack systems to hyperscalers and is an Nvidia ecosystem partner for the 800VDC data center architecture.

On that note, LITEON recently participated in Nvidia GTC 2026 from March 16-19, where it showcased a portfolio of AI data center solutions designed for the Vera Rubin platform, including power systems, advanced rack systems, and liquid cooling technologies. Vera Rubin racks are based on NVL 72 and 144 configurations for Oberon and Kyber racks, respectively. While these racks will mainly utilize copper for scale-up and CPO for scale-out, Nvidia shared during its GTC keynote that Oberon racks can scale up to 576 GPUs with Rubin Ultra GPUs.

Scaling up to 576 GPU clusters requires CPO since copper interconnects will not be able to meet the required performance and bandwidth demand as electric signals over copper degrade rapidly, limiting copper interconnects to distances less than 1 meter. Furthermore, Nvidia’s next-gen Kyber racks for the Feynman platform, which will be available in 2028, can be scaled up to 1152 GPUs using CPO to create a massive supercomputer.

This architectural shift presents LITEON with an opportunity to offer rack systems supporting these configurations by utilizing the 1.6T transceivers made under its partnership with POET for scale-up to hyperscalers utilizing non-Nvidia clusters, such as Meta (META) with its MTIA ASICs and Amazon’s (AMZN) AWS’ Trainium ASICs. Given that copper interconnects are expected to dominate scale-up through 2028, LITEON could start selling rack systems including this 1.6T transceiver by late 2027 as a bridge to CPO, potentially setting POET up for a meaningful revenue ramp from this partnership.

In addition to the LITEON partnership, POET announced an expansion to its existing partnership with Lessengers during OFC, where both companies will co-develop 1.6T 2×DR4 optical transceivers. These transceivers will utilize POET’s Teralight optical engines to integrate lasers, modulators, photodiodes, and passive optical components and Lessengers’ patented Direct Optical Wiring (DOW) technology to route light between photonic devices and fiber interfaces.

Unlike the LITEON partnership, the Lessengers partnership offers a shorter-term path to revenue, as samples of these transceivers will be available in Q2, meaning that customers could start qualifying these transceivers almost immediately. This offers a path to start receiving purchase orders by the end of the year, potentially setting POET up to start high-volume manufacturing in early 2027 for transceivers made under this partnership.

With that in mind, the sampling of this transceiver would represent the second instance of POET’s Teralight engine being sampled by customers, given the ongoing sampling of its 1.6T receive engine co-developed with Semtech (SMTC) in late September. The receive engine integrates Semtech’s FiberEdge 200G/lane transimpedance amplifiers (TIA) on POET’s optical interposer to improve electrical and optical signal integrity at 200G/lane.

While the ongoing sampling of the receiver engine can prove that Teralight can receive data at 1.6 Tb/s, the successful sampling of the transceivers made co-developed with Lessengers would prove that Teralight can transmit data at 1.6 Tb/s, which is the more challenging battle.

The main challenge facing 1.6T transceivers is the laser itself, which is the most expensive and heat-intensive part of the engine. Traditional 1.6T transceivers require 8 laser chips for 2x200G/lane architectures or 16 laser chips inside 1x100G/lane architectures, all of which generate heat.

The presence of this many heat-generating lasers within a transceiver could undermine the reliability and lifespan of a transceiver and thus require effective cooling solutions, representing an additional cost for hyperscalers. At the same time, lasers are extremely sensitive to temperature, as any fluctuations in the temperature of the transceiver could drift their wavelength and degrade their light current curve and potentially lead to crosstalk that could undermine the signal integrity.

These challenges are addressed by Teralight since it is based on POET’s optical interposer platform that eliminates wire bonds. Additionally, Teralight was co-developed with Mitsubishi Electric and thus utilizes Mitsubishi’s 400G Electro-Absorption Modulator Laser (EML). As a result, Teralight only has 4 laser chips instead of 8 or 16, with each EML handling two 200G channels simultaneously, effectively cutting the number of the most expensive component inside a transceiver.

Another advantage Teralight’s architecture has is that its lower count of laser chips helps address a critical bottleneck in 1.6T transceiver manufacturing, the tight supply of InP. InP is the backbone of high-power lasers, as it is a direct bandgap material that is efficient at converting electricity into light. InP’s properties are crucial for 1.6T optical transceivers as it naturally emits light in the range between 1310nm to 1550nm, which is where optical fibers have the lowest signal loss, allowing data to travel long distances without fading.

Since InP is the only material that can handle 1.6 Tb/s speeds efficiently, there is a global shortage of the material. Currently, global demand for InP substrates is near 2 million, whereas global production capacity is 600 thousand substrates, meaning that there is a 70% gap between supply and demand. Against this backdrop, POET’s Teralight engines could have a substantial moat by allowing module makers to produce more transceivers with the same global InP supply.

Based on this, a successful sampling for the transceivers co-developed by POET and Lessengers would be a significant validation of POET’s optical interposer platform, as it would prove that 1.6T optical transceivers can be made at a much lower cost compared to traditional transceivers. In this way, I expect POET to receive purchase orders related to the Lessengers-POET transceiver late this year, as the sampling process typically takes around 6 months.

POET’s Nvidia-Parallel Ecosystem

Given the aforementioned InP shortage impacting laser supply, a major tailwind for POET is Nvidia’s latest investments in Coherent and Lumentum. On March 2nd, Nvidia invested $2 billion in each of Coherent and Lumentum for R&D support and capacity buildout. These investments also came with nonexclusive agreements, including multi-billion purchase commitments from Nvidia and future capacity access rights for advanced lasers.

With Nvidia effectively locking up substantial future advanced laser capacity, POET could be favorably positioned to fill this potential supply vacuum thanks to its partnerships with LITEON, Luxshare, and Foxconn Interconnect Technology (FIT), all of which are a tier behind Coherent and Lumentum as suppliers to hyperscalers. As a result, POET could start seeing a meaningful revenue ramp in the second half of 2027.

With that in mind, POET’s current largest revenue opportunity could be from Luxshare, which started sampling the company’s 400G FR4 and 800G 2xFR4 Infinity receive optical engines in February 2023. In August 2024, both companies expanded their partnership, where POET started providing Luxshare with transmit and receive optical engines for 400G and 800G pluggable transceivers for 100G/lane and 200G/lane architectures.

The engines that Luxshare sampled with POET were the same engines included in the company’s $5 million purchase order received last October for delivery in the second half of this year, suggesting that the customer is very likely to be Luxshare. Considering the speculations that Luxshare is a potential major supplier of 800G transceivers for Meta, which is estimated to require 15 million 800G transceivers this year, this purchase order could be the first field trial for POET’s products in a hyperscaler’s environment and potentially set it up for more meaningful orders in the first half of 2027.

On another note, POET could be set to receive a purchase order from FIT for its Starlight ELS product by the end of the year. During OFC, FIT showcased an upgraded version of its 102.4T external laser small form-factor pluggable (ELSFP), designed for Broadcom’s (AVGO) Tomahawk-6 CPO architecture.

FIT’s ELSFP utilizes a 1310nm CW DFB laser to deliver 26 dBm output power per channel and supports 16-channel 1.6T applications. This upgraded version builds on the early version that was capable of achieving 20dBm of output power per channel. The specifications of FIT’s ELSFP align with POET’ Starlight benchmarks, which could be a sign that Starlight is the light engine powering FIT’s ELSFP module.

Starlight is a DFB laser that supports 4, 8, and 16-channel configurations and has shown the capability of running at 20dBm output power per channel at OFC. Starlight’s capabilities are made possible due to being based on POET’s optical interposer platform that monolithically integrates splitters and muxes. This allows Starlight to handle 16 channels of high-power CW light without significant thermal issues while maintaining total power consumption below 10W as FIT intends.

Given Starlight’s potential for powering FIT’s 1.6T ELSFP modules, POET could have a substantial opportunity from FIT being reportedly named the primary supplier of Nvidia’s Kyber racks at GTC 2026. Kyber racks will be first introduced with Vera Rubin Ultra as an NVL 144 system, utilizing copper for scale-up and 102.4T switches instead of pluggable transceivers for scale-out.

These switches require ELSFP modules, such as the one FIT showcased at OFC, to emit light into these switches. Based on this and POET’s existing partnership with FIT dating back to 2024, the Starlight ELS could be a crucial component in Kyber racks sold by FIT to hyperscalers, presenting another potential opportunity for meaningful revenue ramp for POET in late 2027.

With POET partnering with Tier 2 ODMs, it appears that its moves over the past years have created an ecosystem parallel to Nvidia’s. Through this ecosystem, the company is ensuring that its products are crucial components in hyperscalers’ data centers, considering that hyperscalers are actively reducing their reliance on Nvidia’s complete, closed-loop solutions, either by designing their own ASICs or diversifying their AI infrastructure.

In a world where Nvidia has effectively locked up advanced laser supply from Coherent and Lumentum, this network could be a significant moat for POET as it provides module makers with a viable laser source alternative to Coherent and Lumentum. To that end, POET is well capitalized to expand its manufacturing capacity to ensure it meets this potential demand.

POET’s War Chest Keeps Growing

Since my last coverage of the stock, POET raised an additional $142.3 million in net proceeds from the sale of 20.7 million shares last January, bringing its cash position to $451.8 million after taking into consideration the $700 thousand payment related to the SPX acquisition on October 31st. Additionally, POET has 43.2 million warrants outstanding that could add another $224.7 million to its cash balance if exercised while increasing its outstanding share count to 195.9 million.

At its current market cap of $792.6 million, POET holds 57% of its market cap in cash. This suggests that the stock is undervalued relative to its cash holdings, in my opinion. In Q3, POET reported $107.8 million in total assets and $41.4 million in liabilities, resulting in a book value of $66.4 million. When taking into consideration its capital raises since then as well as the SPX payment, the company’s total assets would be $466.8 million compared to total liabilities of $40.7 million, implying a book value of $426.1 million.

Based on this, POET trades at a 1.86 P/B at its current $5.19 share price compared to the sector median of 3.29, suggesting a 77% valuation gap to the sector median. While POET’s pre-revenue status and dilution history are the main contributors to this gap, I expect it to close if the company shares positive commercial updates, especially at its upcoming Q4 update.

Risks

While the vision behind POET’s strategy over the past years is starting to unfold, the main risk to my thesis is the concentration of POET’s potential customers and key suppliers. As mentioned earlier, a key differentiator of POET’s Teralight engines is Mitsubishi’s 400G EML that allows the engine to only have 4 laser chips instead of 8 or 16.

As such, if Mitsubishi faces yield challenges or is impacted by the InP shortage, POET may be unable to fulfill potential orders for the Teralight engine, potentially limiting the upside from its LITEON and Lessengers partnerships. Similarly, POET’s current revenue-generating opportunities are limited to Adtran (ADTN), the customer behind its first $500 thousand purchase order received last September, Luxshare, and Celestial AI.

The current concentration of POET’s customer base exposes it to risks related to extended qualification timelines for its customers’ products. In that case, the company’s volume production timelines could be pushed from 2027 into 2028, delaying its expected meaningful revenue ramp.

Finally, investors should note the dilution risk associated with POET, which has consistently taken advantage of higher share prices to raise capital. Moreover, the 43.2 million outstanding warrants could be a headwind to POET’s share price if it moves on good news, considering that the exercise of these warrants would increase POET’s outstanding share count by more than 28%, despite the potential for increasing its cash holdings by nearly 50%.

On that note, the majority of these warrants are out-of-the-money, as 37.4 million warrants have a weighted average conversion price of $5.71, of which 13.6 million have an exercise price of $7.25. While this fact limits short-term dilution risk from warrant exercises, it is still a risk worth monitoring over the coming quarters.

Conclusion

POET is uniquely positioned at the intersection of a global InP shortage and an architectural shift to 1.6T optics. By enabling a parallel ecosystem of Tier 2 ODMs, FIT, Luxshare, and LITEON, the company is positioning itself to have its products at the core of hyperscalers’ data centers that rely on non-Nvidia AI clusters. With Nvidia locking up future supply of advanced lasers from Coherent and Lumentum amidst a global InP shortage, POET’s reliance on 4 laser chips only for its Teralight engines could be a key differentiator for its engines by enabling module makers to manufacture more transceivers with the same global InP supply.

Furthermore, POET could have a role in Nvidia’s Kyber racks through FIT, which has reportedly been selected as the primary supplier of these racks. As is, the specifications of FIT’s 102.4T ELSFP module, that is required for scale-out, aligns with the POET’s Starlight ELS, suggesting that its light engine is likely powering FIT’s ELSFP module. With a strong cash position allowing for significant capacity expansions, I expect POET to start receiving meaningful orders by late 2026, positioning it for a major revenue ramp in late 2027. It is for these reasons that I’m reiterating my strong buy rating for POET.

data.title: Innovative Aerosystems: Strong Buy Despite Growth Headwinds This Year
data.description:
Innovative Aerosystems (ISSC), previously known as Innovative Solutions and Support, has gained 78.7% since my last report. The stock price reached as high as $30.94 indicating a 157% gain, before retreating to the current price levels. In this report, I discuss the company’s Q1 2026 earnings and update my price target, explaining why the stock remains a strong buy in my view even after a 240% return since I initiated coverage.

Commercial Aftermarket Drives Earnings Growth

Sales increased by 36.5% to $21.8 million, driven by a $5.5 million increase in commercial aftermarket sales partially offset by a $1.5 million reduction in sales for the F-16 program driven by production transitioning to the company’s Exton facility, which boosted sales last year, and a $0.5 million decrease in business aviation sales driven by transitioning the Pilatus to its new autonomous flight platform UMS-2.

Services sales increased $2.3 million to $8.2 million, driven by inorganic growth in IRU and radio product sales partially offset by a decrease in legacy customer service revenues. What we are mindful of is that the company’s growth for roughly half was driven by inorganic growth. One element that is often underappreciated but was a driver of organic growth during the quarter is the developer role that Innovative Aerosystems has. The company saw tailwinds from software upgrades, which are high margin, and there are not many companies that can develop those upgrades and have them certified.

Gross profit increased to $11.9 million compared to $6.6 million a year ago, with gross margins hitting 54.5% compared to 41.4% a year ago, driven by volumes, mix within the commercial aftermarket, and the mix of commercial against defense aftermarket and OEM sales. Defense tends to bring long revenue tails but against lower margins.

Operating income increased from $1.3 million to $6.3 million as operating expenses increased by roughly $0.3 million against a sales increase of $5.8 million at higher margins. Free cash flow improved from $1.6 million to $7.1 million, reflecting the strong growth in the sales performance.

The Reality Of Innovative Aerosystems Growth

For the second quarter, the company has guided for $20-$22 million in sales and flat organic growth for the full year. It shows that the company in some way depends on M&A to post strong growth. The acquisition of Moog’s (MOG.A) S-TEC Model 3100 Autopilot product line fits in that inorganic growth strategy. The response to this acquisition was a 30% decline. Details of the transaction are not disclosed, but we note that on the 17th of February, the company filed for the sale of up to $100 million worth of shares, which could be used to finance the acquisition.

While the company posted strong growth margins of 54.5% in Q1, the company continues to expect mid-40% margins over the course of the year. The reason is that the first quarter benefited from higher commercial aftermarket activity driven by some early shipments. Those shipments do not occur again in the second quarter, and generally commercial margins are substantially higher than defense and OEM margins. We also note that the ability to show strong year-on-year growth is somewhat limited, as the company benefited from a pull forward in F-16 program revenues in preparation for the company to port production to its Exton facility. The company has moved production to its facility in Q4 FY25 with all products recertified, putting it on a more normalized production cadence in 2026, which is at a lower rate compared to last year when sales were boosted to facilitate the transition while still meeting customer commitments. Some investors believe that the F-16 is a sunsetting program. It is not. It is not the most advanced fighter jet, but it is still being built by Lockheed Martin (LMT) bringing in some OEM sales, and sustainment will last decades. The revenues may eventually decline, driven by a shrinking fleet, but historically the economics of mature or late cycle programs bring higher margins.

So, what we are overall left with is a year that has a tougher comparable period due to more normalized F-16 revenues and quarterly revenues also not benefiting from significant growth absent of favorable timing of order and material receipts, and that does not only affect the company’s top line but also the gross margins.

Innovative Aerosystems Remains A Strong Buy

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions, and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For Innovative Aerosystems, I am maintaining my strong buy rating. There are several reasons for that; the first one is that the company remains undervalued against its median EV/EBITDA against 2027 earnings even when implementing a $100 million capital raise to finance future acquisitions. Furthermore, we note that the stock remains undervalued against the aerospace and defense peer group. In terms of growth, we do expect annual EBITDA growth of 8.1% to trail the sales growth of 10.1%, but free cash flow is expected to increase substantially this year. My price target for Innovative Aerosystems is $24.20 indicating 13% upside with 39% upside when looking at earnings for the year after, while the stock is currently fairly valued, offering an attractive entry point for a business that should see a combination of longer-term organic and inorganic growth.

Margins are expected to decline this year and next year, as 2025 was an unusually high-performance year driven by front-loading some business to facilitate the F-16 transition. The company is expected to have a very strong cash conversion while the net debt leverage is manageable. It even shows a net cash position, but that is driven by the fact that we did implement a $100 million capital raise, but without transaction values known, we are not assigning a cash outflow related to the acquisition, and we are also not increasing EBITDA and FCF estimates that may come with the acquisition.

Conclusion: ISSC Stock Remains Compelling

For Innovative Aerosystems, we note that organic growth is expected to be weaker, as last year was a year with front-loaded business, and we also see a longer-term margin mix pressure as the business grows, but through the defense programs, the margin mix will be lower. That is not an indication of diminishing demand, but the business is simply transforming itself to be a mix of cyclical commercial programs and less cyclical defense programs. The former comes with higher margins, while the latter comes with lower margins but more streamlined revenue tails. The current turmoil due to the war may eventually pressure the commercial business, but for now I am maintaining my strong buy rating as I also assess stocks on their longer-term appeal.

data.title: Global Business Travel: A Stock To Watch With Strong Upside Potential
data.description:
Background

I wrote the article "Global Business Travel Group: Buy This Travel Stock On Revenue Growth" in August 2023. My investment thesis was driven by transaction volume recovery, margin improvement, and the return to positive free cash flow analysis. The stock moved higher to $9.45 but has since pulled back to $5.33 due to macroeconomic concerns and ongoing CWT integration uncertainty. In this article, I revisit the thesis using FY2025 results and analyze whether the stock has upside potential.

In my view, Global Business Travel Group (GBTG) is a financially improving company with strong revenue growth, expanding margins, growing free cash flow, and an accretive CWT acquisition. The stock is trading at 6.26x forward EV/EBITDA compared to the peer average of 9.12x. With timely execution of the CWT integration and improving macroeconomic indicators, the stock has potential for upside.

Positive Growth Outlook for the Business Travel Sector

During COVID, businesses thought that virtual meetings would be a new way of working. However, later they realized that in-person meetings are critical for negotiations, team building, and exploring new business opportunities. This realization leads to a sustained structural recovery for the business travel industry. According to the American Express Global Business Travel Trendex survey, more than 95% of senior executives are bullish about the sector and expect business travel to remain the same or increase over the next year. The same sentiments were also echoed by the independent industry reports confirming the positive trend of the long-term growth outlook.

  • According to Grand View Research, the global business travel market is projected to reach $3.2 trillion by 2033 from $1.8 trillion in 2025, growing at a CAGR of 7.4% from 2025 to 2033.
  • The IMARC Group projects the global business travel market to reach $2.0 trillion by 2033, growing at a CAGR of 6.3%, supporting further positive growth over the long-term trajectory.

From the above analysis, we can conclude that the global business travel market is growing and not saturated post COVID. The growth can be attributed to digitalization and the adoption of AI-powered travel management solutions.

The industry is broadly divided into managed business travel and unmanaged business travel. GBTG operates in the managed business segment (i.e. corporate travel managed by a company), which represents the largest segment. The companies are looking for managed business travel to optimize and control travel spending in an environment of rising travel costs and macroeconomic uncertainty. As a prominent player, GBTG is well positioned to increase its market share of this growing market. In a nutshell, the addressable market provides a strong positive trend with sustained top-line growth, and GBTG has both the scale and the product capabilities to capitalize on this opportunity given its scale and product offering.

CWT Acquisition - The Key Catalyst Investors Should Watch Closely

Since my last article on GBTG, the CWT acquisition is the key development, as this deal makes GBTG a significantly larger player in managed corporate travel globally and well positioned to grab higher market share from the growing global travel business industry.

From the perspective of impact on financials, the deal will improve the operating margins, as management has expected $155 Mn in cost synergies projected over three years driven by consolidation of operations.

With our CWT synergies and AI-powered cost savings, we believe we have a significant cost optimization opportunity that will drive material margin expansion over the medium term" said Karen Williams, Chief Financial Officer of GBTG.

Beyond cost synergies, the CWT deal will make the combined entity a truly global player. Before the CWT acquisition, GBTG's penetration was less in Europe and Asia-Pacific. In a nutshell, the CWT deal has a margin and market share investment thesis that will have a significant impact on the performance of the company over the years.

Revenue Acceleration - Main Driver Is The CWT Acquisition

In Q4 2025, the company generated a revenue of $792 million, which increased by 34% compared to the previous year. The most important thing for investors to see is that the revenue growth without CWT is just 8%. On volume metrics, transaction growth was 37%, and TTV grew 45% in Q4 2025. The growth in both key metrics was driven by the acquisition of CWT, market share gains, and business travel demand. Further, adjusted EBITDA increased by 17%. In terms of annual guidance for FY 2026, management has a double-digit growth expectation for revenue, adjusted EBITDA and Free Cash Flow highlighting that CWT is an accretive deal. For investors, tracking post-deal progress closely is important. The most promising acquisitions also carry integration risk. Technology integration takes time, and any delays during the transition would be a negative catalyst for the stock. The Q4 2025 customer retention rate stands at 96%. Any drop from this level is a red flag, and investors should watch this metric carefully in every upcoming earnings report.

Peer Comparison — GBTG Is the Cheapest Name In The Group

Peers EV/EBITDA (Forward)
Global Business Travel Group (GBTG) 6.26x
Booking Holdings (BKNG) 11.61x
Expedia Group (EXPE) 7.46x
Trip.com Group Limited (TCOM) 8.30x
Average Multiple 9.12x

Based on my analysis, GBTG is trading at 6.26x forward EV/EBITDA. That is a 45% discount to the peer average of 9.12x. The current stock price is $5.33 and based on the comparable analysis, it could have an upside potential of ~$8.00 over the next 12 months. On the sell-side, the view is broadly positive. Evercore ISI recently raised its price target to $12.00 from $10.00. UBS and BTIG both carry price targets of $10.00. The recommendation is consistent for a buy rating. However, investors should be extremely cautious about the investment risk due to the integration execution of CWT and the ongoing geopolitical situation. Any prolonged geopolitical conflict will affect corporate travel demand and negatively impact GBTG's performance.

Final Thoughts

GBTG is a financially improving company with record revenue, expanding margins, growing free cash flow, and an accretive CWT acquisition. It operates in a business travel market projected to reach $3.2 trillion by 2033, with more than 95% of senior executives expecting travel to remain the same or increase. With positive industry tailwinds and improving fundamentals, the stock is still trading at 6.26x forward EV/EBITDA compared to the peer average of 9.12x. Hence, the stock has the potential to move higher. However, investors have to watch the CWT integration closely for a few quarters.

data.title: 18 Ideal 'Safe' Buys In March Sustainable Dividend Test
data.description:
Foreword

A Dogcatcher test of 55 Attractive Sustainable Dividend Dogs (ASDD) found 27 in the safe zone where free cash flow yield is greater than dividend yield. And out of these 27 safer dogs, 18 showed the ideal dogcatcher first-time buyer condition where dividends from $1K invested exceed their single-share price.

[See a summary of the top ten fair-price March ASDD (below actionable conclusion #21 mid-article) and the eighteen Dogcatcher IDEAL choices in the Afterword, at the bottom of this article.]

Actionable Conclusions (1-10): Brokers Expect 25.34% to 55.51% Net Gains From Top-Ten ASDD by March 2027

Four of the ten top ASDD (tinted gray in the chart below) were among the top ten price gainers for the coming year based on analyst 1-year target prices. So, this June 2025 yield-based forecast for them, as graded by Wall St. wizard estimates, was 40% accurate.

Estimated dividend returns from $1000 invested in the ten highest-yielding stocks and their aggregate one-year analyst median target prices, as reported by YCharts, created the 2025-26 data points for the projections below. (Note: one-year target prices estimated by lone analysts were not applied.) Ten probable profit-generating trades projected to March 2027 were:

NewtekOne (NEWT) was projected to net $555.06, based on the median of target price estimates from 6 analysts, plus the estimated annual dividend, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 25% over the market as a whole.

Graphic Packaging Holding Company (GPK) was projected to net $518.78, based on dividends plus the median of target price estimates from 11 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 35% under the market as a whole.

Copa Holdings, S.A. (CPA) was projected to net $457.08, based on dividends plus the median of target price estimates from 15 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 11% under the market as a whole.

Medallion Financial Corp. (MFIN) was projected to net $436.41, based on dividends plus the median of target price estimates from 2 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 22% less than the market as a whole.

Best Buy (BBY) was projected to net $285.36 based on the median of target price estimates from 20 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 46% over the market as a whole.

Reynolds Consumer Products Inc. (REYN) was projected to net $279.44, based on dividends plus median target price estimates from 7 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 43% less than the market as a whole.

OP Bancorp (OPBK) was projected to net $267.65, based on dividends plus the median of target price estimates from 2 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 34% under the market as a whole.

Donegal Group Inc. (DGICA) was projected to net $254.98, based on dividends plus the median of target price estimates from 2 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 95% under the market as a whole.

First Merchants Corporation (Indiana) (FRME) was projected to net $253.49, based on the median of target price estimates from 6 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 7% less than the market as a whole.

UGI Corporation (UGI) was projected to net $253.39, based on dividends plus the median of target price estimates from 2 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed 9% greater than the market as a whole.

The average net gain in dividend and price was estimated at 35.62% on $10k invested as $1k in each of these top ten ASDD stocks. This gain estimate was subject to average risk/volatility 7% less than the market as a whole.

The Dividend Dogs Rule

Stocks are dividend dogs when they show three key qualities: (1) paying reliable, repeating dividends, and (2) their prices fall. Therefore, (3) yield (dividend/price) grows higher than their peers. Thus, the highest-yielding stocks in any collection become valued as "dividend dogs." More specifically, they may be best named "underdogs," owing to their contrarian nature.

Top 55 March 2026 ASDD by Analyst Targets

Top 55 March, 2026 ASDD by Yield

Actionable Conclusions (11-20): 10 Top March ASDD Stocks by Yield Ranged 4.93% to 6.77% Per YCharts

The top ten ASDD by yield occupied four of eleven Morningstar sectors.

The first of four financial representatives took first place, NewtekOne [1]. The other three placed seventh, eighth, and ninth: Medallion [7]; First Interstate (FIBK) [8]; and Farmers National (FMNB) [9].

The first of two industrials sector members was second, Global Ship Lease (GSL) [2]. The other placed fifth, Copa Holdings [5].

Three utilities representatives placed third, sixth, and tenth: Suburban Propane Partners (SPH) [3], Star Group, L.P. (SGU) [6], and Edison International (EIX) [10].

Finally, a lone consumer discretionary firm was fourth, Best Buy [4], to complete the March ASDD top ten dogs by yield.

Dividend vs. Price Results

The graph above shows relative strengths of the top ten March ASDD by dividend and price as of market close 3/25/26.

This month, just two of the top ten dividend-paying ASDD by yield show less than ideal status because the price exceeds the projected annual dividends from $1k invested in each.

A dividend dogcatcher's priority is to select stocks whose dividends from $1K invested are greater than their single share price. As mentioned above, that condition was reached by all but two of the highest-yield ASDD: NewtekOne, Global Ship Lease, Suburban Propane, Best Buy, Star Group, Medallion Financial, First Interstate, and Farmers National.

The two most overpriced of the top ten, Copa Holdings and Edison International, showed costs per dividend dollar under $24.00.

55 Attractive Sustainable Dividend Dog Costs Per Dividend Dollar as of 3/25/26

The full chart above reveals ASDD Dividend Dogs subjected to the same test: divide the price of each stock by the annual dividend. Thus is calculated the cost per dividend dollar for each stock.

The least expensive dog of the March ASDD pack is NewtekOne at $14.25 per dividend dollar, and the most expensive is Business First Bancshares at $44.63 per dividend dollar.

Actionable Conclusion (21): Six of Fifty-Five ASDD Show Negative Free Cash Flow Margins

A negative gap between dividend yield and free cash flow yield defines a cash-poor stock. That is, their dividend payout exceeds their cash on hand to pay dividends. Six ASDD so infected are Suburban Propane, Edison International, Graphic Packaging, RGC Resources, UGI Corp., and Radian Group. These are considered unsafe to buy.

Remember this dogcatcher yield-based stock-picking strategy is contrarian. That means rooting for (buying) the underdog is productive when you don't already own those stocks. If you do hold those stocks, then you must look for opportune pullbacks in price to add to your position to best improve your dividend yield. Plenty of pullback opportunities appear to be ahead. Dread the Fed, uncertainty, wars, deals, confusion, and tariffs, too!

Price Drops or Dividend Increases Could Get All Ten Top Yield ASDD Back to "Fair Price" Rates for Investors

The charts above retain the current dividend amount and adjust the share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal or exceed the single share price of each stock. As the top illustration shows, eight of the top ten are already ideally priced. Besides those seven already in the ideal zone, two more top-yield stocks are within $32.40, or 29%, of getting there.

The alternative, of course, could be that those two companies raise their dividends or do stock splits. That may be too much to ask in these highly disruptive, inflationary, recessionary, yet cash-rich, and volatile times. Mr. Market is far more effective at moving prices up or down to appropriate amounts; just watch and buy when your targeted stock price moves to a sweet spot.

Actionable Conclusions: (22-31) Broker 1-Yr. Targets Showed 22.21% to 49.73% Top Ten ASDD Upsides to March 2027; (32) One -16.05% Downsider Trailed

To quantify top-dog rankings, analyst median price-target estimates provided a "market-sentiment" gauge of upside potential. Added to the simple high-yield "dog" metrics, analyst median-price target estimates provided another tool to dig out bargains.

Analysts Forecast a 6.71% Advantage for 5 Highest-Yield, Lowest-Priced of 10 ASDD as of March 25, 2026

Ten top ASDD were culled by yield for this monthly update. Yield (dividend/price) results as verified by YCharts did the ranking.

As noted above, the top ten ASDD selected 3/25/26 by yield represented four of eleven sectors. Communication Services, Consumer Staples, Energy, Health Care, Materials, Technology, and Real Estate failed to make the top-ten cut.

Actionable Conclusions: Analysts Expected 5 Lowest-Priced of Ten Highest-Yield ASDD (34) to Deliver 23.01% vs. (35) 22.42% Net Gains by All Ten Come March 25, 2027

$5000 invested as $1k in each of the five lowest-priced stocks in the top ten Dividend ASDD by yield were predicted by analyst 1-year targets to deliver 2.65% more gain than from $5,000 invested in all ten. The second lowest-priced top ten yielding stock, NewtekOne, showed top analyst-estimated gains of 55.51%.

The five lowest-priced top-yield ASDD dogs for March were Medallion, NewtekOne, Star Group, Farmers National, and Suburban Propane, with prices ranging from $8.49 to $20.25.

Five higher-priced ASDD top-yield dogs as of March 25 were: First Interstate, Global Ship Lease, Best Buy, Edison International, and Copa Holdings, with prices ranging from $33.63 to $115.29.

The distinction between five low-priced dividend dogs and the general field of ten reflected Michael B. O'Higgins' "basic method" for beating the Dow. The scale of projected gains based on analyst targets added a unique element of "market sentiment" gauging upside potential. It provided a here-and-now equivalent of waiting a year to find out what might happen in the market.

Caution is advised, however, since analysts are historically only 15% to 85% accurate on the direction of change and just 0% to 15% accurate on the degree of change.

As bullish euphoria in the market dies and the feckless Fed-fueled slide and dithering presidential tariff, wars, and deal pronouncements gain momentum into 2026, with the exception of a few meme stocks, look for all of the top twenty ASDD to become fair priced…slowly, maybe suddenly, but surely.

Afterword

Buy 18 Ideal Out of 27 'Safer' March Dogs of the Attractive Safer Dividends

In late March 2026, 27 of the 55 ASDD were tagged "safer" for dividends as they showed positive one-year returns and free-cash-flow yields greater than dividend yields. YCharts 3/25/26 data showed nine as fair-priced: Global Ship Lease, Star Group, Medallion Financial, First Interstate, Provided, Norwood, Donegal Group, First Hawaiian, and PCB Bancorp.

How ASDD 'Safer' Dividend vs. Price Results Produced "Fair Price" Results for Investors

The chart at the top above reveals that nine dividend stocks from $1K invested in our top ten 'safer' dividend ASDD conform to the dogcatcher standard of being equal to (or greater than) the price of a single share. Those nine ideal dogs were listed previously.

The middle chart shows the recent dividend amount, and the bottom chart adjusts that share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) equal to or greater than the single share price of each stock—a so-called, "fair price."

A market correction of only 29% could put all ten share prices of these "safer" survivors above or equal to their dividend payouts from $1k invested.

There are, of course, other ways for dividends from $1K invested to exceed the price of a single share of stock. That would be for the board of directors of Copa Holdings to raise dividends or do stock splits to achieve the dogcatcher ideal level. That's a lot to ask with all the corporate funds doing so much "good" elsewhere.

Stay tuned for future Dividend Dogcatcher Sustainable Dividend updates, out here, in public, away from the pay-walled Seeking Alpha Investing Group site (formerly known as the Marketplace).

Footnote

It All Comes Down to Eighteen IDEAL "Safer" Attractive Sustainable Dividend Dogs

The net gain estimates mentioned above did not factor in any foreign or domestic tax problems resulting from distributions. Consult your tax advisor regarding the source and consequences of "dividends" from any investment.

Stocks listed above were suggested only as possible starting points for your "Safer" Attractive Sustainable Dividend stock research process. These were not recommendations.

Disclaimer: This article is for informational and educational purposes only and should not be construed to constitute investment advice. Nothing contained herein shall constitute a solicitation, recommendation, or endorsement to buy or sell any security. Prices and returns on equities in this article, except as noted, are listed without consideration of fees, commissions, taxes, penalties, or interest payable due to purchasing, holding, or selling same.

Graphs and charts were compiled by Rydlun & Co., LLC from data derived from Indexarb, YCharts, finance.yahoo.com, and analyst mean target price by YCharts. Open-source dog art from dividenddogcatcher.com.

data.title: Wintrust Financial: Premium Franchise, But Macro Concerns Keep Upside Capped
data.description:
Thesis

Wintrust Financial (WTFC) is a high-quality player in the regional banking space that has demonstrated superior loan growth, robust deposit growth, and a brilliant multi-charter structure model. However, severe macroeconomic uncertainty related to the Iran war taking interest rate cuts by the Federal Reserve off the table, concerns around the private credit market, and overall credit deterioration in the economy make the upside limited. Consequently, I am issuing a hold rating on WTFC.

Company Profile

Wintrust is a financial services company headquartered in Rosemont, IL, with over $60 billion in assets. The firm operates through 175 community bank locations and offers traditional and commercial community banking, wealth management, mortgage origination, commercial insurance premium financing, and short-term accounts receivable financing. Wintrust operates in the Chicagoland area, southern Wisconsin, and northwest Indiana. Wintrust operates under a unique multi-charter community banking model with 16 separately chartered community banks. Wintrust had $71 billion in total assets, $53 billion in total loans, and about $57 billion in total deposits as of Q4, 2025.

Fourth Quarter, 2025 Earnings

Wintrust reported a strong finish to 2025 with Q4, 2025 earnings that produced an 11% increase in net income year-over-year from $525.1 million in Q4, 2024 to $583.9 million in Q4, 2025. This increase in net income was driven by growth in average earning assets and improved net interest margin. Net Interest Income (NII), the difference between the interest the bank earns on its assets, like loans and investments, versus the interest the bank pays out on its liabilities, like deposits, increased $583.9 million in the fourth quarter. This metric is up $16.9 million from the third quarter, driven by the growth in average earning assets.

The company also reported strong balance sheet growth, with assets increasing $1.5 billion in the fourth quarter, driven by loans and liabilities increasing $1.3 billion, driven largely by organic deposit growth. Finally, on the credit side, the Provision for Credit Loss (PCL), an income statement item that accounts for losses on troubled loans, jumped $16.9 million from $567 million in the third quarter to $583.9 million in the fourth quarter of 2025. Management attributed these "qualitative attributes" added to the provision related to credit spreads and equity market valuations per the Q4, 2025 earnings presentation.

Digging deeper into the rise in the PCL shows that nearly the entire increase was caused by a line item titled "Provision for unfunded lending-related commitments losses - Other," while the amount allocated to actual loan losses (Provision for loan losses - Other) actually dropped from $19.6 million in Q3, 2025, to $14.3 in Q4, 2025. Unfunded lending-related commitments are lines of credit like commercial revolvers and construction loans. These are approved loans that have not yet been drawn on by the borrower.

The significant increase in PCL allocated to this item ties to our macroeconomic risks because it shows management is preparing for stressed middle-market and corporate borrowers that may need to quickly access lines of credit to shore up liquidity due to a deteriorating macroeconomic environment. While this can be seen as a proactive step by management to prepare for potential weakness in the economy, it does warrant our attention. Despite the issues revolving around the PCL, Wintrust's Q4, 2025 earnings demonstrated a strong organization that is well-managed and exploiting a strategic advantage with the community-focused business model.

Return Metrics

The first return metric we will examine, and arguably the most important, Net Interest Income. Wintrust has demonstrated strong growth in net interest income from Q1, 2024, to Q4, 2025, with a growth rate of about 26% over the time period. The growth in NII over this time period is largely attributed to growth in Wintrust's average earning assets, with a $5.5 billion increase in loans. This level of NII growth shows the firm's strategy of acquiring assets is working well and producing strong returns.

Net Interest Margin (NIM) measures NII divided by average earning assets. This metric tells us how much NII the bank is generating from its interest-earning assets like loans and investments. Wintrust has consistently produced NIM in the mid-three percent range, demonstrating the bank is generating healthy NII from its earning assets. For reference, the peer median NIM as of Q4, 2025 was 3.13%. Moreover, Wintrust sits at the top of its peer group in terms of NIM as of Q4, 2025, at 3.52%. This metric further reinforces the strategic capabilities of Wintrust and its management.

The final return metric we will examine is Return on Tangible Common Equity (ROTCE). This metric assesses the net income generated from the bank's tangible common equity, that is, the common equity after removing intangible assets like goodwill, which can arise from non-core activities like acquisitions. This metric gives us valuable insight into how much income the bank is generating from its capital base. Over the prior eight quarters, Wintrust's ROTCE has hovered close to the peer median of 15%. These values for ROTCE show that Wintrust is competitive in terms of using its capital base to generate income for shareholders.

Company Structure

As mentioned earlier, Wintrust operates under a unique multi-charter community banking model. Wintrust operates under 16 separately chartered community banks, giving it local branding, decentralized decision-making, and deep community ties. This differentiates Wintrust from cookie-cutter community banks in that each of the 16 banks has its own local board of directors and its own localized loan committee. This type of local banking creates brand loyalty and "stickier" deposits that may be less rate-sensitive than those of competitors. Perhaps the greatest advantage of Wintrust's structure is their unique MaxSafe deposit program. Since Wintrust has 16 separate bank charters, a client can spread out deposits across the charters and receive 16 times the standard FDIC insurance limits, or $4,000,000. This increased insurance limit gives clients additional peace of mind, particularly after the events of 2023, when we saw deposit runs on major banks.

Balance Sheet and Credit Quality

Turning to the balance sheet, Wintrust reported exceptional loan growth across all major loan categories in 2025, growing loans to $53.11 billion in 2025 from $48.06 in Q4, 2024, representing about a 10% increase. A large portion of this increase came from commercial and commercial real estate loans. Wintrust has consistently delivered solid loan growth going back to 2015, growing at a compound annual growth rate of 12% for the prior 10 years. This level of growth shows the company's business development efforts are sound and provide strong results.

On the deposit side, Wintrust grew deposits to $57.72 billion in Q4, 2025, up from $52.51 billion in Q4, 2024, representing an approximate 9.7% increase. The firm's strategy of acquiring deposits has yielded a 12% compound annual growth rate over the past 10 years, showing that the strategy is working well. Management attributed the deposit growth in 2025 to targeted marketing efforts, a diverse product offering, and gains in market share, partially attributed to the Macatawa acquisition completed in August 2024. This robust growth in deposits shows the firm is able to capture deposits at an excellent rate to further fuel growth.

Moreover, non-interest-bearing deposits are the cheapest source of funding for banks, as they allow the bank to lend out nearly zero-cost funds to generate loans. Wintrust's ratio of non-interest-bearing deposits to total deposits has consistently exceeded 19%, a quality level in the regional banking industry. This ratio of non-interest-bearing deposits further demonstrates Wintrust's sound balance sheet and growth strategies.

On the credit quality side, we will examine two critical metrics for banks: net charge-offs to average loans, which measures the dollar value the bank has written off for bad loans net of recoverables to its average loans, and non-performing loans to total gross loans, which measures the ratio of troubled loans to the bank's total loans.

Net charge-offs to average loans tell us the portion of the bank's loans that are being written off. Over the prior eight quarters, net charge-offs to average loans averaged 0.18%, with a brief spike in Q2, 2024, resulting from "gradual normalization of credit metrics" per management. However, upon further inspection, the jump in net charge-offs in Q2, 2024 can largely be attributed to the commercial real estate portfolio generating a $15.5 million charge-off with only $90,000 in recoveries. While the downward trend from Q2, 2024 is promising, the net charge-offs should garner attention in the future, with about 26% of the loan book being allocated to commercial real estate.

Next, we will examine non-performing loans (NPLs) to total gross loans, which gives us insight into the ratio of Wintrust's troubled loans versus its total loans. Over the prior eight quarters, NPLs to total gross loans have averaged 0.36%, meaning less than 1% of the bank's loan book are considered problem loans. The stability of non-performing loans to total gross loans over time can be attributed

Macroeconomic Environment

Wintrust has demonstrated excellent growth metrics in 2025 and remains a high-quality player in the regional banking sector. However, we must also be cognizant of the macroeconomic environment the company operates in and the fact that regional banks are very cyclical; that is, they are sensitive to the business cycle. The current headwinds cannot be ignored and make entering into new positions a bleak endeavor at this time. The headwinds are as follows:

Regional banks perform best when the yield curve is steep and upward-sloping, in other words, when rates on the short end are lower than rates on the long end of the curve. As of the time of this writing, the market has essentially priced out all rate cuts that were priced in prior to the Iran war. Per the CME FedWatch tool, fed funds futures are pricing an approximate 71% probability of no cuts, only a 3.7% chance of one 25 basis point cut by then, and even 25% in rate hikes.

Additionally, the deterioration in the private credit market has elevated fears of a contagion throughout the broader market. Regional banks are particularly exposed to this potential contagion as the primary lenders to private credit funds. In fact, the Federal Reserve stated that credit lines by U.S. banks to private credit vehicles increased about 145% over the prior five years in its note titled "Bank Lending to Private Credit: Size, Characteristics, and Financial Stability Implications." These private credit funds then use "back leverage" to enhance returns. This practice refers to borrowing against a pool of loans from banks, which can work well in good times but may sting when times are bad.

The issue is particularly acute for regional banks, as these types of loans make up a larger portion of their balance sheets relative to the big banks, per a research piece from Capital Advisors titled "Are Regional Banks Sitting on A Private Credit Time Bomb?" For instance, the research piece specifically cites a loss linked to Tricolor for $178 million at Fifth Third (FITB). The loans to private credit could cause significant issues for regional banks in the event of an economic stress scenario if pressure from investor redemptions forces them to rely more heavily on bank credit lines for liquidity at the same time collateral values may be falling, causing banks to pull back on these facilities, further stressing the private credit funds.

In case this wasn't enough of a cause for concern, a stress in the private credit market may threaten bank capital if the credit quality of these loans suddenly falls. Banks are required to hold regulatory capital in proportion to their risk-weighted assets; a larger portion of "high-risk" assets generally forces a bank to hold more capital. While these loans to private credit funds may currently be considered "low-risk," if the credit quality deteriorates, they may be elevated to high-risk, forcing banks to shore up more capital to meet regulatory capital requirements, which would mean less capital deployed to grow the business.

Finally, signs are emerging of stress in consumer credit as evidenced by three key points. First, credit card delinquencies (90+ days past due) are approaching prior cycle peaks at 7.18%, per the St. Louis Fed's Quarterly Report on Household Debt and Credit. Additionally, student loan balances that have entered serious delinquency have risen to 16.19%, which may force borrowers to prioritize payments on these loans at the expense of other loans.

Lastly, stress is emerging in the Buy Now, Pay Later (BNPL) market as a report from LendingTree titled "BNPL Tracker: 41% of Users Late in Past Year, More Using Loans for Groceries" shows that 41% of users have paid late on these loans, up from 34% a year ago. While Wintrust does not specifically provide financing for these types of loans, broad credit issues like this can impact banks as consumers may have to forego payments on other loans.

Valuation

For valuation, we will use the price-to-tangible book value (P/TBV) and the price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio for backup.

First, the P/TBV ratio is the preferred metric for banks as it controls for intangible assets like goodwill, which can arise through non-core activities like mergers, which are common in the banking industry. On a P/TBV ratio level, WTFC trades at an approximate 2% discount to its justified price of $138.32 based on the peer median of 1.56 and the current price of $135.33 as of the time of this writing.

Second, the P/E ratio is a suitable backup to the P/TBV ratio; however, it is important to note that this metric can be distorted by the PCL, which we reviewed earlier. On a P/E ratio level, WTFC trades at almost a 3% premium to its justified price of $131.36, based on the peer median of 10.43.
