This shows why it is helpful to have a backup metric for valuing complex firms like banks.

Taking the midpoint of the justified price from the P/TBV ratio and the P/E ratio, we arrive at a fair price of $138.32, or an approximate 2% upside from the current price of $135.33. Suffice it to say this is well within the margin of error for calculating fair value; therefore, Wintrust appears to be trading at its fair value.

Conclusion

Wintrust is a high-quality player in the regional banking space that has experienced strong growth excellent deposit growth and uses a multi-charter business model that has grown the firm significantly. However, a yield curve squeezing net interest margin, the possibility of a private credit contagion, and falling demand for loanable funds make this the wrong time to enter into new positions on regional banks. Consequently, I am issuing a hold rating on Wintrust.

data.title: Funko: Avoid This Stock As Sales Pull Back Further
data.description:
In tough macroeconomic environments, weak companies often sink. Amid oil-driven inflation and a still-weak consumer spending environment, we're cautious about struggling companies like Funko (FNKO), the maker of Pop! collectible figurines.

Since the start of the year, shares of Funko have lost ~15% of their value, the culmination of a very tough year for the stock in which it has lost about 60% of its value over the past twelve months. The question for investors now is, is this a chance to buy the dip in Funko, or is the company permanently damaged?

I last wrote a sell article on Funko in December, when the stock was trading in the low $3s. Since then, the stock has proceeded to lose value. At the same time, it's hard to point to anything in its fundamentals that can justify a recovery. I remain at a sell rating here.

To me, there are quite a few red flags that this company faces that investors should be fully aware of. The core risk factors are:

  • Funko is facing a very weak sales environment. Revenue has declined at a persistent double-digit pace. The company is facing weak consumer spending and a cautious retail partner environment as well. Also, since Funko focuses on current trends and fads, incorrectly planning supply could cause issues with obsolete inventory. Inventory write-offs have recently crimped the company's gross margin profile.
  • Tariffs are eating into gross margins. Funko already suffers from relatively weak <50% gross margins that will ultimately cap its ability to scale. Tariffs are pushing down margins further, with ~4 points of negative impact in the most recent quarter.
  • Moderately indebted. The company is carrying about $224 million in gross debt ($182 million in net debt) that sits at a ~2.4x leverage ratio. Amid falling sales and waning profitability, the company doesn't have much flexibility if sales slip further.

It's true that Funko's valuation is modest in nominal terms, but considering the operational risks the company is shouldering, I find its multiples to be fairly priced at best. At current share prices just shy of $3, Funko trades at a market cap of $162.5 million. After we net off the $42.1 million of cash and $224.1 million of debt on the company's latest balance sheet, Funko's resulting enterprise value is $344.5 million.

For FY26, Funko is guiding to 0-3% sales growth and $70-80 million in adjusted EBITDA, or an 8.1% margin. We caution that this represents vast improvement over 2.9% margins in FY25 (though not quite yet at FY24 levels at a 9.0% margin), despite the fact that sales deleverage, obsolete inventory, and tariffs will still factor in as headwinds next year.

Nevertheless, if we take this guidance at face value, Funko trades at 4.6x EV/FY26 adjusted EBITDA. Again, this isn't a terrible price to pay, but considering the recent slippage in sales and various operating risks in a tough economy, this isn't a bet I would make.

Q4 Recap

Let's now go through Funko's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Funko's revenue declined -7% y/y to $273.1 million, which did beat Wall Street's expectations of $260.7 million (-11% y/y) by a four-point margin. Revenue trends did improve somewhat relative to a -15% y/y decline in Q3.

Still, it's a long road to recovery for Funko. The chart below showcases Funko's sales trends by product line. As shown below, the company saw flat trends in its Loungefly accessories business. However, core collectibles saw a -$12 million decline in revenue. Certain properties did well: the company notes that sales of items branded for KPop Demon Hunters and Stranger Things, both of which are core recent titles on Netflix (NFLX), did well. That said, overall sales weakness was still pronounced, especially in the U.S. The company also separately buckets sales losses from SKUs that it has discontinued in its "Other" line, though I continue to disagree with this categorization (as I'm of the belief that if these SKUs had been present, they might have cannibalized against products in the core collection).

Unfortunately, however, Funko's profitability has been trending weaker. As shown in the chart below, pro forma gross margins fell -150bps y/y to 40.9%.

The good news is that underlying product margins improved 370bps y/y, while a greater mix of full-price DTC sales has also helped the company's margin profile. Unfortunately, this was entirely offset by -410bps of negative headwind from tariffs. On top of this, the company suffered a -140bps hit to gross margins from inventory reserves: essentially unsold inventory that the company has had to mark down. As previously mentioned, due to Funko's reliance on current trends to drive sales of figurines, weak demand and poor supply planning could continue to be a major drag on the bottom line.

Adjusted EBITDA also fell -11% y/y to $23.3 million, falling to an 8.5% adjusted EBITDA margin that reduced -40bps y/y.

For now, the company is fairly confident that it doesn't need to dip into additional debt to fund its declining profits. Per CFO Yves LePendeven's remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

So the answer is no, we don't expect to need to do any additional borrowing. We're managing right now on our operating cash flows and expect to continue to do so. We make regular quarterly principal and interest payments on the debt, and we plan potentially later in this year and certainly early next year to make some incremental debt paydowns like we did in Q4."

Yet we note that free cash flow in FY25 was -$38 million, and the company has only $42.1 million of cash left on its books. If the weak macro pushes sales trends even lower in FY26, we may see Funko enter into a deeper cash crunch.

Key Takeaways

While Funko's low-single-digit adjusted EBITDA multiples look tempting, I believe Funko is a classic value trap. The company hasn't been able to solve its multi-quarter problem of declining sales while facing a tough macro and eroding margins. Steer clear here and invest elsewhere

data.title: Argan: At Highs, But Strong Earnings Momentum Keeps The Bull Case Intact
data.description:
The Thesis

Exiting FY26, a key provider of power and industrial infrastructure services, Argan, Inc. (AGX), posted a strong double-beat result in Q4, reflecting continued demand momentum and solid project execution. While demand for the company's capabilities remains robust across the power infrastructure, with rising data center investments and electrification trends, additional support from a strong $2.9 billion backlog should drive top line growth through FY27.

For margins, I believe focusing on disciplined execution on large-scale projects and operational efficiencies should be a key growth factor over the coming quarters, supporting bottom line growth in FY27 and beyond as the top line expands. Since my last bullish rating, the AGX stock has given strong double-digit returns, driven by solid earnings performance in Q4.

While the recent rally has expanded the valuation notably and now looks relatively stretched on FY27 earnings estimates, it still appears reasonable due to the company's strong earnings momentum, robust growth outlook, and favorable position in an industry with long term structural tailwinds. The risk-reward indeed looks more balanced at the present levels, but I believe the company continues to offer a compelling opportunity for long-term investors, supporting a continued buy rating on the stock.

AGX’s Q4 FY26 Highlights

Earlier this week, AGX reported its final quarter results for FY26, closing the year strongly with approximately 12.7% year on year growth across the company’s consolidated top line, reaching $262 million in Q4 FY26. This growth was primarily driven by the completion of certain large projects during the quarter, which benefited the revenue in the company’s core Power Services segment.

Although a smaller contributor, AGX’s Industrial construction segment also benefited the company's revenue growth notably, expanding by about 61% to $52.9 million during the quarter. For the full year, the consolidated top line was up 8.1% versus FY25, inching closer to the billion-dollar mark, at approximately $944.6 million.

While the company’s top line continues to expand, it also remains focused on strong project execution, which significantly benefited its profitability during the last quarter. As a result, the company’s adjusted EBITDA margin jumped about 450 bps to a record 21.4% in Q4. EBITDA growth also meaningfully boosted the company’s bottom line, as its adjusted EPS jumped nearly 60% year on year to $3.47, beating the estimates by a notable $1.49 in Q4 FY26.

Comments on Outlook

After experiencing a slight moderation during the middle of FY26 due to unfavorable project timing, AGX closed the year on a solid note with an impressive Q4, primarily driven by healthy growth in the core Power service segment. While there was some temporary softness driven by project timing earlier, a solid Q4 reinforces the company’s underlying growth trajectory. I expect this momentum to continue into FY27 as the demand environment for the company’s capabilities across the segments remains strong.

The power services segment, which remains the core contributor with about 80% share in total revenue, continues to see strong demand from increasing requirements for new power generation capacity across the region. While AI-led data centers continue to expand rapidly, I expect the requirement for new capacity to rise further to serve the need for reliable baseload power infrastructure across North America.

This strong demand environment can also be seen in the company’s project activity throughout FY26, with new contracts during the year reaching $2.5 billion. These include both natural gas and renewable projects. However, about 77% of them are natural gas related, which includes three large gas-fired power plant projects in the U.S. with a combined capacity of about 3.4 GW. With this strong order activity, the company’s consolidated backlog stands at approximately $2.93 billion as of January 2026, which provides solid visibility for the company’s top line growth as it progresses through FY27.

Additionally, the company also has many large projects that are progressing steadily. These include the 1.2 GW combined-cycle natural gas project in Texas and a 700 MW combined-cycle gas-fired project. While these projects are advancing through their later stages, I expect them to further contribute to the company’s revenue growth meaningfully over the coming quarters and support the company’s consolidated top line in FY27.

AGX’s outlook beyond FY27 also looks equally promising due to structural tailwinds from rising investments in data centers, electrification trends across industries, and onshoring of manufacturing. Given years of underinvestment in power generation capacity across the region, the need for new and reliable energy infrastructure is increasing. This is likely to fuel a multi-year investment cycle in power infrastructure across North America, which should significantly benefit companies like AGX due to their strong execution capabilities and expertise in complex projects.

AGX also has a long-standing customer relationship with a great track record. In addition to its capabilities, I believe strong customer relationships should further strengthen its position in capitalizing on future project opportunities over the coming years.

Furthermore, AGX also continues to focus on enhancing its capabilities and expanding its footprint through strategic M&As. With approximately $895 million in cash and equivalents and absolutely no debt, the company’s financials also remain strong, which provides it with the flexibility for future M&A opportunities, which should further benefit its top line in FY27 and beyond.

Valuation Update

I last covered the AGX stock in December 2025, after it reported its Q3 results for FY26. While the stock corrected nearly 20% post-earnings release amid concerns around top line weakness and a revenue miss in Q3 earnings, it was still trading at a forward multiple around 38x its FY26 earnings at that time.

Despite the short term softness, I remained bullish, as the weakness appeared largely project timing-related rather than structural. And, as expected, the company delivered a strong double beat in its Q4 FY26 earnings, significantly exceeding Wall Street expectations, leading to a sharp post-earnings rally of about 38%. As a result, the stock is now up about 74% since my last buy call, significantly outperforming the S&P 500, which has been down 7.5% during the same period.

However, after the recent surge, AGX no longer appears to be an undervalued growth story. The company's stock is currently trading at a forward non-GAAP P/E ratio of 51.92, based on its FY27 earnings estimates of $10.91. While the stock appears significantly overvalued versus its five-year forward average multiple of around 23x, it is important to note that AGX is not a typical EPC company. But it is positioned as a more focused pure play EPC company with limited direct peers and strong capabilities.

Plus, the valuation is also supported by strong forward growth expectations, driven by a robust project backlog and a favorable demand environment. Due to these factors, I believe the market may continue to assign a premium multiple to AGX relative to traditional EPC peers as long as it continues to execute efficiently and sustain double-digit growth over the medium term.

Moving ahead, AGX's top line outlook appears favorable as strong backlog levels and ramp-up of large projects keep the visibility strong into FY27. Meanwhile, with continued focus on strong execution and improving project management effectiveness, the company's margin should also remain strong over the coming quarters.

Importantly, the company's debt-free balance sheet should also help the company translate the benefit from top line and margin expansion largely into bottom line expansion, which should benefit the company's stock valuation in the future. Additionally, I also believe that the probability of further upward earnings revision in consensus earnings estimates also remains high, given strong and consistent earnings momentum, which is also likely to help normalize the current multiple over time.

Overall, at the present levels, the risk reward appears more balanced after the recent rally, and near term upside may be relatively limited. But, given the company's strong earnings momentum and position in a high-growth market, I still believe that AGX remains a high-quality compounder for the long term.

Risks

Similar to my previous article, lumpiness in AGX's top line remains a key risk to the thesis. As we have seen before as well, these uneven project timings could negatively impact the company's top line in the short term despite solid underlying demand. In addition, project execution risks, including the cost overrun in large-scale projects, also remain a key factor to watch.

Also, as the stock is currently trading at a premium valuation, there is almost no room for any execution issues. Given the elevated expectation, if the company could not deliver as expected in the coming quarter results, or growth moderates, the company's stock might see a near term correction..

Final Verdict and Rating

As discussed above, the company's stock is now trading at a stretched valuation following the recent surge. The company's outlook for FY27 appears solid with strong project visibility and strong margin levels, which should continue to support earnings growth over the coming quarters. Although the risk-reward looks more balanced now, AGX's strong market positioning and strong execution continue to support a constructive view, given a promising long term outlook. Therefore, while some caution is warranted at present levels, I remain bullish on AGX and believe that any short-term volatility should be viewed as an opportunity for investors with a long-term view rather than a concern. Hence, I am maintaining a "BUY" rating on the AGX stock.

data.title: Capital One's Series I Preferred Stock Offers Decent Yield And Potential Capital Gain
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Capital One Financial Corporation (COF) has several outstanding series of preferred stock (all of which are pari passu), including two non-publicly traded series. Their five series of publicly traded preferred stock each consist of depositary shares representing 1/40th interest in a share of fixed-rate, non-cumulative, perpetual preferred stock, as shown in the following table.

The Series I Preferred Stock (COF.PR.I) is trading at a higher yield (and thus a lower price) than is reasonable for its risk level, particularly as it is trading significantly below its liquidation preference of $25 and, with the highest coupon of Capital One's publicly traded series and the presently callable series, is the most likely of the outstanding preferred stock series to be called anytime soon (particularly if the Federal Reserve cuts rates, thus opening up the possibility of refinancing this series at a cheaper coupon). This implies a good chance of significant capital gains if it gets called. Given the major, global economic shocks in the commodity and financial markets related to the new war on Iran as well as the chaotic on-again/off-again trade wars, there is risk to COF's consumer-based lending if the economy falls into recession (Q4 2025's GDP growth was revised downward from 1.4% to 0.7%). While this may impact Capital One's common stock's fortunes on the downside (as I discuss in a recent review of Capital One's common stock), COF.PR.I should be relatively shielded from impact on its dividend. I rate COF.PR.I as a buy.

Company Overview

Founded in 1988 and headquartered in McLean, Virginia, Capital One Financial Corporation (the "Company") is the holding company of Capital One, NA, a bank primarily focusing on consumer and commercial banking. The company's customer deposits fund its lending, with its loan-to-deposit ratio ("LDR") ranging from 90% to 96% over the past five years (at the end of FY2025, after the merger with Discover, LDR was 96%). According to its profile on Seeking Alpha, the Company operates over three segments: Credit Cards, Consumer Banking, and Commercial Banking. Deposits consist of savings accounts, checking accounts, money market deposits, negotiable orders of withdrawal, time deposits, and sweep accounts. It offers credit card loans, personal loans, auto loans, retail banking loans, commercial and multifamily real estate loans, and commercial and industrial loans.

The largest event impacting the Company over the last five years was its $35.3 billion acquisition of Discover Financial Services, finalized on May 18, 2025. It was an all-stock deal for 1.0192 shares of COF for each Discover share. This was followed by a notice on January 22, 2026 that the Company was acquiring Brex for a combination of stock and cash worth $5.15 billion. While the Company may very well benefit in the long run from these acquisitions, for the near term it appears that the Company will have a tough climb to return to its five-year historical average return on equity ("ROE") of 11.53%.

Review of the Series I Preferred Stock

As shown in the following table, COF.PR.I have the highest coupon (5.0%) of all five of Capital One's publicly traded preferred stock series outstanding. It also exceeds the coupon of the non-publicly traded Series M preferred stock. Only the non-publicly traded Series O preferred stock has a higher coupon (5.50%), but its call date is not until October 30, 2027.

COF.PR.I's annual dividend of $1.25 is paid out on a quarterly basis (March 1, June 1, September 1, and December 1), the same as Capital One's other preferred stock series except Series O, which is paid twice yearly until October 30, 2027, after which it will be paid quarterly. At $18.60, Series I is currently trading at a 25.6% discount to its liquidation preference of $25.00. This is a lower discount than the other four publicly traded series, which ranged from a 29.4% discount to a 37.0% discount. COF.PR.I's current yield of 6.720% is just below the other publicly tradable preferred stock series. This is likely because it has the highest coupon and is also the most likely callable series of Capital One's publicly traded preferred stock.

All five of the publicly traded preferred stock series are fixed rate, non-cumulative, perpetual preferred stock. In order for preferred stock to be counted as Tier 1 capital, it must be non-cumulative, so financial institutions generally issue non-cumulative perpetual preferred stock. As with all non-cumulative preferred stock, dividends are paid only when, as, and if declared by the respective institutions' board of directors. These dividends have precedence over the dividends paid to common stockholders.

Capital One's Ratio Analysis

I took a look at historical ratios (calculated using Capital One's 10-Ks and 10-Qs) to give me an indication of how Capital One has performed, which will show the safety of COF.PR.I's dividend and its liquidation value of $25 per depositary share. A series of ratios on profitability, financial strength, liquidity, and efficiency, all using figures as reported, are shown in the following table.

Net interest margin measures the difference between interest income and interest expenses, with a higher margin being better. Capital One has grown its net interest margin annually from 5.66% in FY2021 to 7.40% in FY2025.

ROE shows how much net income is generated by the average common shareholder's equity. Please note, net income is what remains after income attributable to the non-controlling interests (such as preferred stock) is paid out. A higher ROE shows that there is a cushion of earnings to the common equity that is above and beyond the dividends for the preferred stock, thus leaving room for lower earnings to the common shares without sacrificing the dividend of the preferred stock. ROE has declined annually, with FY2021 having an ROE of 21.45% and FY2025 having an ROE of 2.66%. This is significantly below COF's cost of (common) equity of 9.14%, found using the Capital Asset Pricing Model ("CAPM").

Return on Assets ("ROA") measures net income against average total assets of Capital One. This measure also declined annually from 2.80% in FY2021 to 0.38% in FY2025.

The provision for credit losses ratio shows the amount of provisions for credit losses as a percentage of net loans. This number increased annually from 4.21% in FY2021 to 5.43% in FY2025. The provision for credit losses measure also reduces net income and increases the allowance for credit losses, a contra-asset account on the balance sheet.

Several ratios for financial strength were reviewed, with higher percentages showing greater financial strength. The Tier 1 leverage ratio shows a bank's core capital to its total assets. The Common Equity Tier 1 ("CET1") risk-based capital ratio shows a bank's common equity (i.e., exclusive of preferred stock and qualifying minority interests) against its risk-weighted assets. The Tier 1 risk-based capital ratio shows a bank's equity capital and disclosed reserves against its total risk-weighted assets. Finally, total risk-based capital ratio shows the sum of Tier 1 and Tier 2 capital divided by the bank's risk-weighted assets. All these measures for Capital One are significantly above regulatory minimums for all years reviewed.

It should be noted that the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation ("FDIC") approved new rules in November 2025 easing leverage requirements for banks, requiring less capital set aside to protect against losses of lower-risk assets (like US Treasuries).

The company's LDR is a percentage of how much of the company's deposits are utilized to fund loans. Having a ratio below 100% means all the bank's lending is funded internally, whereas a ratio higher than 100% means the bank is dependent on outside financing (e.g., through raising funds from new sales of preferred stock) to finance its growth. Capital One's LDR ranged from a low of 90% in FY2024 to a high of 96% in FY2025. This leaves some room for Capital One to increase its lending backed by deposits.

The efficiency ratio shows non-interest expenses per dollar of revenue, with a lower ratio being better. Looking at FY2021 through FY2024, this ratio ranged from a low of 54.44% in FY2021 to a high of 55.95% in FY2022. In FY2025, it increased to 57.08%.

Revenue growth less non-interest expense growth, when positive, shows that revenue is growing faster than expenses, as opposed to a negative ratio, which shows the company is accumulating expenses faster than it is growing revenues. This figure ranged from a low of negative 13.87% in FY2021 to a high of positive 1.39% in FY2023, only to then decline over the next two years to end at negative 5.33% in FY2025.

As with all preferred stock, COF.PR.I ranks below debt and above the common stock, COF, in terms of liquidation preference and dividend payments. As of December 31, 2025, COF's (i.e., the common stock's) book value per share is $181.76, and its tangible book value (book value less goodwill and intangible assets) per share is $107.72. COF's current market price is $176.16, which has fallen from $241.44 on December 31, 2025. Despite this decline, there is a large cushion of common equity that can be drawn down before resorting to lowering COF.PR.I's equity stake.

In summary, these ratios show that while Capital One fares favorably in its financial strength and liquidity ratios, it has some issues with its profitability as well as its efficiency, particularly after its acquisition of Discover Financial in FY2025.

Preferred Stock Comparables

A sample of preferred stocks issued by financial institutions of similar asset size was reviewed and compared to COF.PR.I. I left out JPMorgan Chase Bank (due to its size), Goldman Sachs (as it has minimal consumer finance business), State Street (it had no outstanding preferred securities), and TD Bank (which is denominated in Canadian dollars, so not comparable to the others). See the following table taken from the Federal Reserve.

I also included a search for preferred securities issued by Capital One's peers, as listed on Seeking Alpha. To winnow down the results, I excluded cumulative securities, floating-rate securities, fixed-to-floating-rate securities, and short-dated rate-reset preferred stock. This search resulted in 26 comparable securities, as shown on the following table.

Whether the preferred shares were trading at a premium to or a discount to their liquidation prices shows whether a higher yield is demanded by the market than the coupon issued by these companies. Most of these preferred stocks traded at a discount to their liquidation preferences, with a handful of preferred stocks trading at a premium to their liquidation preferences. These preferred stocks ranged from a 40.1% discount to liquidation preference to a 2.4% premium to liquidation preference, with a mean and median discount to liquidation preference of 19.4% and 24.1%, respectively. COF.PR.I currently trades at a 25.6% discount to its liquidation preference (slightly above the median).

The yields on these securities ranged from a low of 5.97% to a high of 7.52%, with a mean and median of 6.28% and 6.26%, respectively. COF.PR.I's yield (as traded) is 6.72%. It should be noted that if the single preferred stock issued by Synchrony Financial was excluded from the sample, the maximum yield of the sample dropped to 6.47%, with the mean and median being 6.23% and 6.26%, respectively. That puts Capital One's Series I preferred stock, as traded, at a higher yield than the adjusted sample.

Most of these securities, including Capital One's Series I preferred stock, were already past their call dates and thus had no meaningful yield to call. It should be noted that several securities in the sample had very large yields to call. This occurred when they traded at a significant discount to their liquidation preference and were only a few coupon payments away from their call dates. If called, they would receive the full $25 call price, thus having a large capital gain at call, inflating the yield earned for holding these securities to call.

At $53,434 million, Capital One had lower revenue than all the other financial institutions in the sample, except for U.S. Bancorp, Synchrony Financial, Truist Financial Corporation, and Bank of New York Mellon Corporation. Revenues ranged from $14,981 million to $113,097 million, with the mean and median revenue being $72,339 million (just above Capital One's revenue) and $83,699 million, respectively.

In terms of net income available to the common shareholders (i.e., a cushion to losses that needs to be zeroed out before dividends to the preferred shareholders are cut), it ranged from a low of $3,469 million to a high of $29,055 million, with a mean and median of $17,669 million and $18,267 million, respectively. Capital One's net income available to common shareholders was $2,181 million, i.e., below the group's minimum. This would imply a higher yield for the Series I preferred stock than the comparable sample.

I also looked at the net income margins of the sample, which ranged from a low of 18.89% to a high of 36.41%. This was well above Capital One's net income margin of 7.48%. A similar pattern was seen for ROA and ROE, with the sample's minimums being 0.54% and 6.83%, respectively, while Capital One's ROA and ROE were 0.37% and 2.39%, respectively. These differences would imply that Capital One's Series I preferred stock should trade at a higher yield than the sample.

All these factors should be considered together when determining a yield for a security. In particular, the likelihood of the Series I stock being called before any of the other preferred stocks issued by Capital One implies its yield should be lower than other Capital One preferred stocks. However, given the comparison to the metrics of the sample, the Series I preferred stock should trade at a yield towards the high end of the sample. Taking all of this into account, I selected a reasonable yield of 6.50% for the Series I preferred stock, just below its 6.72% yield it is currently trading at. This implies a preferred stock price of $19.23, a 23.1% discount to its liquidation price. This compares favorably to its currently traded price of $18.60.

Risks & Other Factors

According to Capital One, the Discover acquisition created a global payments platform with over 70 million merchant acceptance points in more than 200 countries and territories. The Company cites $2.7 billion in pre-tax synergies and, by 2027, that it will be >15% accretive to adjusted non-GAAP earnings per share. Their 2027 return on invested capital is projected to be 16%, with an internal rate of return of >20%. These projections are potential positives for the company; however, the company's declining ROA and ROE in the past five years show multiple warning signs that these projections might not come about. Additionally, the company's concentration in consumer lending typically carries a risk of loss that is higher than commercial lending and can be adversely affected by an economic downturn. The likelihood of an economic downturn increases substantially when trade wars heat up, as they are currently, or if the independence of the Federal Reserve comes into question. The war in Iran has also roiled commodity and financial markets, and it is unclear how long this factor will negatively influence the stock market as well as the overall economy.

Conclusion

Given all the preceding information, I believe that even with the potential downside to Capital One's common stock highlighted in my earlier article, its dividends to its preferred securities should be relatively safe. As a result, I estimated a yield for the Series I preferred stock of 6.50% with an implied stock price of $19.23. Any future rate cuts by the Federal Reserve will further boost the preferred stock's price and simultaneously make it more likely to be called, as Capital One could then refinance this higher coupon security for a lower coupon one. I rate COF.PR.I as a buy.
